private static synchronized DateTimeZone fixedOffsetZone(String id, int offset) {
    if (offset == 0) {
        return DateTimeZone.UTC;
    }
    if (iFixedOffsetCache == null) {
        iFixedOffsetCache = new HashMap<String, SoftReference<DateTimeZone>>();
    }
    DateTimeZone zone;
    Reference<DateTimeZone> ref = iFixedOffsetCache.get(id);
    // ...

So now, when offset isn't zero, it checks if false is true, which it never is. That means the initialization of iFixedOffsetCache inside this method is skipped.

2. Some other tests like TestDateTimeZoneCutover#testWithMinuteOfHourInDstChange_mockZone started failing because of a NullPointerException.

But the key point is whether this mutant code change (line 135) is adjacent to any fault points in the original code. 

The term "fault-adjacent" usually refers to code that's near a known bug or an area where errors are likely to occur. In this case, the mutation is within the fixedOffsetZone method, which handles creating DateTimeZone instances based on offsets.

Looking at the failing tests after mutation, it seems that not initializing iFixedOffsetCache when offset isn't zero causes issues elsewhere because other parts of the code rely on this cache being properly set up. For example, testWithMinuteOfHourInDstChange_mockZone now throws a NullPointerException, possibly because DateTimeZone.forOffsetHoursMinutes() or similar methods are trying to access the cache but it's null.

So, the mutant change affects the initialization logic, which is crucial for the proper functioning of DateTimeZone instances. This suggests that the mutation is indeed adjacent to fault points in the original code, as removing the cache initialization introduces new bugs.

Therefore, the answer should indicate that the mutant is adjacent to fault points.
